speaker: interviewer
text: --Farrington in New York City, and this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Helena Bernzweig and previously Radzicki. I am delighted that she came to visit me from Florida. And she's staying with me for a few days just before I am leaving for the United Kingdom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very glad to see you, sweetheart. You know, all my life there's only you and you. And everything that is my power, I would help you, and I would give you and I am doing. You was very sick. I was the one [? saver. ?] I took you away, took you to Germany alone, just like I would schlep you, yes? And I make you feel better and you was better. In to today, god forbid something happened, I would be right there right away to [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE], my love.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you left your-- your child at this door step--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left my child, I don't know what happened because I fainted. And one man, I don't know even his name, he used to take me and to go, because this was the-- the-- the neighborhood. I didn't know. This-- they used to live in another part in Holland. Well, he helped me. I was carrying the child. I put that letter, you know, with the picture I make then I hide. And it was written in this letter was my husband taken to Dachau from the intelligentsia. And they looking for me. So honest people, they don't do-- they don't throw children away. I love her with my whole heart and bottom. No, I-- I have to do this because I want to save her life and my life. And please, if I would be alive, I will take her back. And if I wouldn't survive, you adopt her and bring her up good person. And I put a cross also on the heart not to find out that she's a Jewish baby. She looked very beautiful, blonde. And I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that you would see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [LAUGHS] I didn't know. I didn't-- really, I didn't know. And I always hoped I would see her. I always got my picture from her with me. And God help me, I find her through-- through-- through my relatives. The same young lady, she got the passport from my husband. And she run also away. And being there in [INAUDIBLE] I think. She's heard of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This child was [? thrown ?] away, and she took it. And she knows because before I did, I was in that house. So she looked, and she recognized. And she remembered where-- where this is. And after she come to Germany, I was already there. And she-- we was communicate, not the Jewish [? nor ?] Christian. So she said, your baby is alive. And she is in-- in this-- in this [INAUDIBLE] or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: --[INAUDIBLE]. And-- and-- and when you-- when we would survive, you go there. You find her. So I survived. And even the [INAUDIBLE]. She was in May 10. I come back to Krosniewice. And I didn't have even apartment. I didn't have where to live. And I don't like the way that this [INAUDIBLE]. There was very, very, very bad. This was the Kielce-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kielce, was this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kielce, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start to make right away, in beginning, pograms for the Jews. And a lot of Jews was, this time, killed. And I-- and I went there not as Jew, not-- Catholic, I was a Christian. Because I don't want to say who I am. They could kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went three days what I could come there. I come, finally. I walk around. I see it's a big field with a lot of ships. And an old lady was sitting, watching, taking care--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the-- on the-- on the ships. I went ahead, I said to her, like, like, the-- the Catholics say, [SPEAKING POLISH]. [LAUGHS] This is in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, who are you? I said, maybe you will remember. I took the picture. You could [INAUDIBLE] remember who is the child. You saw her. She looked. She looked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, oh, this is Roma. So I find out [? that ?] her name is Roma. So I said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, where is Roma living now? Could tell me? So she said, oh, not far. In the-- in this is a red building. And go to this building. And there, you'll find the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. I knocked the door. Nobody answered. I opened the door. Come in, seeing on the table a album. I was nosy. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And opened the album, and I recognized my baby. She hardly change. She was one year and two months. And when I come, she was four years. And I looked and I looked. I says, oh my god, this is my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after, one lady comes in. And she says, who are you? I says, don't be scared. I'm the lady what I threw, three years ago, my baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my baby. And I heard her name is Roma. So I'm her mother. I lived through everything, and I'm her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Says, what? Oh, no. I don't have the baby. [INAUDIBLE] my sister. And [? you ?] never, never would get back the baby. I said, OK. I would see. And please, I said, tell me, where is she? I wouldn't tell you. I stay, and I beg her. No. So, what I should do? I go out, and I went back to the subway, to wait to go--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the train. Standing there, I was waiting. A elderly lady comes. She looks at me. She says, lady, lady, do you-- the mother, [INAUDIBLE]? I says, yes. Who are you? I'm the grandmother. My daughter took the baby. You have here, she said, a letter. And go with this letter. And she would be very happy to talk to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I took the letter, what I had with me. I gave her a-- a thank you, for appreciation. And after, she said she's in Lodz. She moved. So I went to Lodz. And I find [? out ?] the time as my sister, one survived. And-- and I find out from the Joint the address. And I went to her apartment. She was survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine, she sawed me. I saw her. After the war, we didn't know from each other to we are alive. The screaming, the crying, the-- the hugging.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and I said, I have my baby. And I don't know where this is. And I have a letter. So I said, I wouldn't go with this letter just what she said. I opened the letter. And I want to read what is there. Maybe is [? against. ?] So I opened the letter. It was beautiful. The mother said, please. God sent you and you in so bad situation now. And-- and-- and-- and go along with this lady. She will take back.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good. So I went, my sister and I find my [INAUDIBLE] [? was ?] survived. And we went there. She was in-- in [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] Street. Well, I come in. I knock the door. My little baby is [SIGHS] sitting on the sofa. I run. I start to hug her, to kiss her. My god. She-- she looks like I will be today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she said, what is the matter, what is she doing to me? And she start to cry to the other woman, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] what-- what's she want? And we all cried. And I hug her and I hug her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after, we sit down, quiet. And I said, now, I'm the mother. I was alive now. And my husband is also alive. And we would like to have my baby back. I would give you as much what I can to help you out over the years and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, all right. Can I go to the church with her now? I says, of course, you can go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after, afternoon, we come later. She went with her to the church. She come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, no. I cannot give you the baby so, like you think. The baby was christened. And she was adopted by me. And-- and-- and now, I says, to what I-- we should do? To go to the court.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I said, I go with you to the court. So, and she find out, they-- they start to-- to talk to her as not to give me [? thought, ?] [INAUDIBLE] me Jews. I'm-- I don't know how rich I am. And she don't know [INAUDIBLE] what she want. And that was-- I went to the court. The court was closed this time. This was the summertime, in beginning of June. And I walk every day, every day to her. And she start to neglect her. She find out that she Jewish and she neglect her very badly. Now, the people what are living there, around, they said, lady, you should take the baby because she neglect her. She's all the time alone. And she was looking so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I don't know what to do. When she saw me from far away, she said, did you bring me something? First she said, [NON-ENGLISH], after, she said lady, after she says, Ma. [LAUGHS] You know, she was confuse. So I, all the time, what I could, I bring it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened-- her husband, I never saw him. Never. He used to belong to the [INAUDIBLE]. This was against the-- the government. Communist, he was a-- a-- a partisan. And they was against this government. So he was hiding all the time. He used to--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: When he-- when he could kill people, he killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he-- he steal to the-- the-- the banks. He was terrible person. He was actually a good man, [? now, ?] from the idea, what he got, was very bad. He was against everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Excuse me. I-- I hate to-- to cut you short. But-- but more of the details of your story, we can get in your individual--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --interview, which we're doing directly after this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We just need to-- to bring this one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in-- in terms of your daughter-- right. To a--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a conclusion.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the end was, I couldn't tolerate no longer. Six week I was surrounding around, after we-- we decide I have to go to the police. Court can be the court, what they-- what they would say, I can do. And I went to the police. And I told them the whole story. And they got very pity on me. And they said, no. You have to have the baby. And you go to the court. And what they would say, you would do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went and we took it with force, her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Terrific.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was, this time, rather with me. I took her to my-- Krosniewice. And as much I could, I want to bring her, you know, to me and to give her love, and everything what was possible, and to start a new life. And after, I was called to the court. And I went. And-- and they-- everybody said that the child belongs to me. And even she brought the-- the-- the clothes what I-- she was wearing, she brought this to the court. And I told them without looking what she was wearing. And this was the end with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Terrific. Well, I want to thank you again very much, both of you, for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --allowing us. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, which I found when I was visiting in Israel. It must have been sent in the '30s. So my father must have been approximately 30 years old. And I was just so delighted to find it because we did not have any pictures of my father at the time, from this time. Everything was lost during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krosniewice. I mean, and it must have been taken winter time, because as you can see, he's wearing a fur-- [CREW] yes. This is a-- part of the cold that you can see. It's a fur. So it must have been in winter. And I think it's sometimes in '30s. I don't know exactly when. But, about maybe '32 or so. In 1932. This is a picture which was of me, Romana Radzicki, taken just few days before my mother left me behind. So, that must have been in 1942. And she kept this picture throughout the war, with her, hidden. After my mother found me and I saw this picture, I decided that-- I had closed eyes. So I just took an ink, and-- and I drew, as you can see, I-- I opened my eyes. I believed that, you know, they should be open. So I must have been, probably, six years old when I did it. I'm sorry I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's was taken in Gielniow, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture, when-- a few days before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gielniow. .

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Gielniow, yes. This is a picture of the family who took care of me, Stanczak. And as you can see, my mother is in-- on the left, and my brother in center, and my father on the right side. And I must-- I must be, here, approximately two and a half years. I was approximately 2 and a half years of age. So it-- it's probably in 1940-- beginning of '44 or end of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother's name. Her name is Gnowefa Stanczak. But I do not remember what was my brother's name and my father's name. For some reason, I think it was [? Stachik, ?] the brother. But I am not sure. [? Stachik, ?] yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but I'm not sure. I hope I find him so I'll find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and again, just to be clear, the only person who's allowed--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, Romana. I must have been, here, approximately two and a half years of age. And I believe we received these pictures from my other mother, Gnowefa Stanczak, right after the war, when my mother got me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it must-- this was taken, definitely, in Kraszkow, at my grandparents' house, in a back-- in a backyard there. This picture was one of the first taken with my real mother after she found me. And it was taken in Krosniewice. And I believe it was taken in our central park in Krosniewice, in 1945. [CREW] I seem like she's really-- she's tickling me. Because I'm laughing. So, that's-- and I think it's a lovely picture of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And-- and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother's-- my mother, Helena Radzicki. Currently Bernzweig. This is a portrait of my family. It must have been done, probably, 1952. Because it looks like my sister's approximately two years old. And this-- her name is Alfreda Radzicki. She still keeps that name, even though she's married. And my mother, Helena Radzicki, and my father, Joseph Radzicki, and myself, Romana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken, definitely, in Krosniewice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can see I look quite awkward as a little teenager. [LAUGHS] It-- it was really done by a professional, I think. Oh, this is a picture of me graduating, I believe, from college, in 1965, from City College. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was done, certainly, in New York City, at the campus of City College. That was taken-- this is a picture of me, Romana, taken in 1969 at-- when I was at NYU medical school, just the day I found out I have a retinal detachment. So I looked pretty sad. But it's a very, very good picture, which I always think of that day, which was a horrifying experience for me. This is of my marriage-- marriage picture to George Logan Farrington, on October 1, 1988. And you can see, I am very emotional. I have tears in my eyes. You can even see that in the picture. [LAUGHS] But-- but I like it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at the United Nations chapel where we got married. That's 777 First Avenue, right across from United Nations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-- I can start?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the verdict from the court, which gave me back to my real parents, Helena and Joseph Radzicki. And the court indicated of what actually was supposed to happen. And one of the things they discussed is that I have to be under the supervision of the Roman Catholic Church till-- till the age of 21. And the next-- the following steps basically enumerate the amounts of money that my parents have to pay for me, for the three years that I have been with the family of Gnowefa Stanczak. And that also includes not only monetary value, but also land. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. OK. Do you know what some of those amounts are?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very large sum of money, [? 50, ?] I believe [? 50,000, ?] what at the time was a large amount of money. But it's not only the amount of money, but also the land, which my father basically gave away to them as well. So, it was both monetary and land value. I feel like a sheep. [LAUGHTER]
